issue_title: Unify rq/celery, remote execution, and PBS feature, and separation of file system features.
issue_body:
In light of the fact that we should also have `path_map`, `to_host` and `from_host` options if the rq/celery workstation and server (redis) are not on the same file system (#438), we should consider unifying all these execution options and treat them as some variant of `hosts`.

Logically speaking:

1. Options `to_host`, `from_host` and `preserved_vars` prepare the step for the execution on hosts with different file systems. They are not triggered if the step is executed locally. These are remote-execution options that are independent of how tasks would be executed on the remote host.

2. Options `walltime` etc (#240) prepare the step for the execution in task queues where estimated resources would be needed. Each variant of the task queue (e.g. PBS, rq) should figure out a way to make use of these runtime options. Again, these are task descriptions and are independent of who will execute the task.

3. The `--queue` option should be an abstraction of job queues when sos submit the tasks. In the `queue` definition, sos needs to know if the workers are on a different file system (configuration `shared`), and how to copy files if that is the case (`path_map` etc). SoS also needs to know how to interact with the queue system (e.g. how to pass runtime option `walltime`). Another key aspect of the `host` is whether or not it is a queue system. SoS would return and check for the status of the job later for a queue system (PBS, rq, and celery), and wait for the completion of the job if it is not (remote execution).

4. Whereas the `queue` option is an global option to send all tasks to certain queues, the `on_host` runtime option should be treated as a per-task specification that provides us an opportunity to send tasks to different queues/servers. It is at least useful for interactive use when we send individual tasks to different servers.

The key question would then how to generalize a good definition of `queue` so that we can work with all these different execution methods. 

1. Remote execution without queue: This is already done and the current `execute_cmd` option seems to be working all right. SoS waits for the completion of the job in this case.

2. `rq`: The rq definition should have `redis` server, `port`, etc. The SoS rq extension would know how to send the job to rq server.

3. `celery`: similar to rq. The configuration file should have definitions for `celery` server. The SoS celery extension would know how to send the job to celery server.

4. `PBS`: ... this is topic of #437.

My understanding now is that we should abstract the `queue` or `host` execution in the SoS core, and implement different types of hosts as SoS extensions.

issue_title: Update __repr__ for transforms
issue_body:
PR https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/380 adds `__repr__` for existing transforms. However, optional parameters have been missed out in the process.

By resolving this issue, I would like to add those optional parameters in the `__repr__` definitions.

For `Normalize`, I noticed that we have to give a sequence. In case we gave a `Number` for either `mean` or `std`, we could effectively elongate it (broadcast), and use it. Currently, if users wanted to normalize with the same value in all the channels, then they would have to create a list containing same entries. I am thinking about a behaviour similar to https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms/transforms.py#L189-L191. 

Please let me know what you think.

cc: @fmassa @alykhantejani

issue_title: `new` command should work with URLs
issue_body:
We should be able to run a command like `pulumi new https://github.com/pulumi/examples/cool-kubernetes`, pointing at some directory with a `Pulumi.yaml`, and install it on a cluster. Initial thoughts are that this would work something like:

1. Get the code at that URL, dump to disk in CWD.
1. Inspect the directory for a `Pulumi.yaml`. Retrieve the `quickstart` member from the YAML object, which gives the engine the information (_e.g._, perhaps simple parameters like name, region, _etc_.) it needs to run `update` and provision the resources.
1. Run `update`.

Possibly there could be a separate command, `install`, which skips step (1) and simply runs `update` on the app.

This additionally would allow us to implement something like the "run on Heroku" badge -- "run with Pulumi" would dump the relevant `pulumi new` command into the clipboard, say, and then the user need only run that on the console, and it should all "just work" to provision those resources.

### Checklist

- [ ] This is a feature request.

### Description

I have got a wrapper command file that invokes streamlink on some models at CB. If the webcam of the model is one  streamlink starts saving the broadcast to a file. However, I have noticed that there are occasional "Unable to open URL" errors: (EOF occurred in violation of protocol (_ssl.c:645)).

### Expected / Actual behavior

If the webcam of a model is not on, streamlink should exit with a "No playable streams found on this URL":  

error: No playable streams found on this URL: https://chaturbate.com/little_mystery/

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test

issue_title: Uses of self.nodes[-1] cause false positives
issue_body:
A little bummed out I didn't realize this until I investigated the failure of the `test_orelse` test on [my ugly branch](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/compare/testing_djangonv#diff-c7870eaa2a6e2a6edee14e0deeccfac6R209), oh well.

Here are the relevant test case and output.

```python
def does_this_kill_us(diff):
	return subprocess.call(diff, shell=True)

@app.route('/poc', methods=['POST'])
def poc():
	try:
	    value = None
	except ImportError:
	    value = request.args.get('foo')
	else:
	    does_this_kill_us(value)
```

output with -trim flag on.
```python
Vulnerability 1:
File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
 > User input at line 9, trigger word "get(": 
	value = request.args.get('foo')
Reassigned in: 
	File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
	 > Line 11: temp_1_diff = value
	File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
	 > Line 1: diff = temp_1_diff
File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
 > reaches line 2, trigger word "subprocess.call(": 
	ret_does_this_kill_us = subprocess.call(diff,shell=True)
```

Where does it connect the basic block of the source to does_this_kill_us? 
That would be in `save_local_scope` where self.nodes[-1] here is the last node that was added, which is `Label: value = request.args.get('foo')`
```python
            previous_node = self.nodes[-1]
            r = RestoreNode(save_name + ' = ' + assignment.left_hand_side,
                            save_name, [assignment.left_hand_side],
                            line_number=line_number, path=self.filenames[-1])
            saved_scope_node = self.append_node(r)

            saved_variables.append(SavedVariable(LHS=save_name,
                                                 RHS=assignment.left_hand_side))
            previous_node.connect(saved_scope_node)
```


Okay, how should we fix it?
Well we currently only use the [-1] node when we're adding a function (in master this means a user-defined function, in my branch it means a user defined function or a blackbox/builtin function) so I guess in say, `handle_or_else` in base_cfg.py we either (a) pass in what should be the node we want to connect to e.g. `value = None` or (b) connect the 2 nodes in e.g. `handle_or_else`. These are both a lot of work though because we have to do something special in everywhere a function can be called.

issue_title: Odd string comparison behavior in python 2.7 vs 3.6
issue_body:
We have a suite of tests that we are trying to convert to use grappa matcher. However, we noticed different test behavior with respect to strings that we haven't seen while using unittest matchers. For instance the following code will pass on `Python 3.6.5`, but not `Python 2.7.10`.

```
import unittest

import requests

from grappa import should


class TestWebRequest(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_web_request(self):
        url = "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
        r = requests.get(url)

        # self.assertIn('Hello World', r.text)
        r.text | should.contain('Hello World')
```

On `Python 2.7.10` we get the following error:

![screen shot 2018-05-25 at 1 41 06 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4236888/40558529-9998779a-6021-11e8-85f3-c7a3305244c9.png)

We can conditionally get it to pass, but encoding the text to `utf-8` before asserting - on 2.7.10, but this might conceal unexpected behavior, for us. Is there any reason for this difference in behavior? Thanks

@TisVictress

issue_title: Method to checksum existing products
issue_body:
#126 removes the `check_existing` option from `download()` and `download_all()`, as it is quite unrelated to the main purpose of these functions. The main use case for that feature was to check the integrity of already downloaded files, which is potentially quite useful.

There are a couple of options regarding this feature:
* Add a separate method for this (`checksum()`?) functionality. Its input could be a list of product ids and a directory or a list of file paths.
* Not implement this at all. The zip files can be verified quite easily with `zip -T` or `7z t` commands or via a GUI.
* Re-add it to `download()`. Since you are likely interested in fixing the broken files rather than just deleting them, `download()` is actually a quite sensible place for this functionality.

The consensus so far in #126 was to make it a separate function.

issue_title: How to use data with dtype=tf.float16?
issue_body:
Due to the large amount and big size of data, I'm considering using `tf.float16` for a higher speed. But all the variables used in all the layers are generated by `tf.get_variable()` with `dtype=None` as default. And simply modifying the input placeholder to `tf.float16` will lead to `tf.cast()` error:
> ValueError: Tensor conversion requested dtype float32 for Tensor with dtype float16

Then I modified all of the `tf.get_variable()` in the layers I use to add the option `dtype=tf.float16`, **including the weight and bias in Conv/DeConv, as well as the `beta`, `gamma`, `moving_mean` and `moving_variance` in BatchNorm**. Any of these above, if left unchanged, will still cause the `tf.cast()` error. I also use Pool, Elementwise and Dropout but they seem to do nothing with `tf.get_variable()`. **However now the training loss become `nan` right after the first batch, though it can run without error at least.**

So what else should I do to run with `tf.float16`? Or is there any other easier approach instead of modifying the TensorLayer code?

Thank you.

if (device().check_app("packageName")==True):clear_app("packageName")

    poco(text="安装").click()

```
